bio.biography:
Colorado Springs Olympic and Paralympic Training Center resident Sophia Herzog was on the U.S. Paralympics Swimming Emerging Team twice in her career before making the national team in 2015...Herzog was first taught to swim and dive by her mother with the Dwarf Athletic Association of America...She swam with Miranda Uhl and Erin Popovich in her first swim meet with U.S. Paralympics...Daughter of Ralph and Patricia Herzog...Interests include riding horses, drawing, painting, skiing and spending time with her dog Goldy...Lives in the town the show South Park is based on...Is a tour guide at the Colorado Springs Olympic and Paralympic Training Center when she is not training
Paralympic Experience
  • 2-time Paralympian; 2-time Paralympic medalist (1 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, bronze (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women), 7th (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women), 15th (50m Butterfly S6 - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, silver (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women), 6th (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women), 10th (50m Butterfly S6 - Women), 12th (400m Freestyle S6 - Women), 13th (100m Freestyle S6 - Women), 14th (50m Freestyle S6 - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – silver (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women), 7th (50m Butterfly S6 - Women), 9th (50m Freestyle S6 - Women)
  • Years of participation: 100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women 2017, 2019, 2022; 50m Butterfly S6 - Women 2022; 50m Freestyle S6 - Women 2017, 2019, 2022; 200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women 2017, 2019; 400m Freestyle S6 - Women 2017, 2019; 100m Backstroke S6 - Women 2017; 100m Freestyle S6 - Women 2017; 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women 2017; 4 x 50m Freestyle Relay 20 Points - Mixed 2017
  • Medals: 6 (2 gold, 2 silver, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2017 (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women, 4 x 100m Medley Relay 34 Points - Women)
    • Silver – 2022 (100m Breaststroke SB6 - Women); 2017 (200m Individual Medley SM6 - Women)
    • Bronze – 2017 (100m Backstroke S6 - Women, 100m Freestyle S6 - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Born with constricted band syndrome
  • Started playing soccer and was active in sports throughout childhood
  • Also played softball and basketball, but eventually chose track and field
  • Her coach suggested she give Para track and field a try, and Heyison was classified and participated in her first development camp in 2021
  • A three-time high school state champion
  • Competes for Wake Forest University at the NCAA Division I level
  • Mother Tanya played volleyball at the University of Maryland - Baltimore County and father Marc was a 9th-round draft pick for the Baltimore Orioles
  • Hobbies include singing and playing guitar, baking and spending time with friends and her dog and cat

Paralympic Experience

bio.education: University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill
bio.biography:
Career Highlights
  • 2004 Olympian
  • 3-time Pan American Champion
  • 4-time U.S. National Champion
  • 2nd at 2008 World Cup - overall standings
  • 2nd at 2008 Oceanic Championships
  • 10th at 2010 Pre-World Championships
  • 2010 National Champion

Recent Results

  • 13th place at 2012 Oceania Open
  • Reached semifinals at the 2012 Australian Open - placing 32nd
  • 2011 US National Slalom Team Member
  • Reached the semifinals at 2011 World Cups Nos. 3 & 4 - placing 19th at World Cup No. 3
  • Reached 2nd round at London 2012 Olympic Test Event
  • Placed 19th in Men's Kayak at 2011 Slalom World Championships

Quick Facts
  • Job: Student-athlete
  • Quote: "Champions are the ones bent over in exhaustion when no one is watching."
  • Role Model: Mia Hamm, she's the greatest ever, inspired millions and humble.
  • Website: www.brettheyl.com

If he were a swimmer or skier you would know him by now. But he is a whitewater slalom kayaker, so you don't. In 2008, he finished 2nd in the World Cup. This was a huge achievement. But being an Olympic year, the only thing people cared about was the Games. He won the 2008 Olympic Trials in Charlotte, NC, but stumbled in the final selection race. Funny enough, it was that same result that secured his World Cup overall medal. It's funny how things work sometimes.

So What's His Story?

These days it takes something pretty remarkable to stand out. Our generation is seeing athletes winning more than 6 medals in single Olympics. We saw a cancer survivor win the Tour de France at will. The Lopez family has 4 family members on the 2008 Olympic Team. Not to mention that every female athlete seems to be a model. The publicity bar has been raised so high that it is nearly impossible to stand out.

He, along with his family have faced adversity, but nothing that handfuls of other athletes haven't endured. No, his story is just the opposite of all of these. These people were handed exceptional situations in which to rise. He is an average person doing exceptional things. He has had to find ways to be better than the rest; ways to stand out, and rise above. That is Brett's story. He's normal. Brett is a testament to what average can achieve. Failing to make the Olympics in 2008 has given Brett his defining moment. Watch Brett between now and London in 2012 as he pushes the boundaries of what an average guy can accomplish as he claws his way back onto the Olympic Team.

What Are His Best Results?

His most notable achievement was racing for the USA in Athens at the 2004 Games. However, this is not what he is most proud of. While being an Olympian is certainly a defining title, he is hoping that it is not the end-all of sport. Being a 4x National Champion, 3x Pan-Am Champion, winner of the 2008 Olympic Trials and World Cup overall medalist are much more important to him. The variety of titles shows his consistency, and ability to race well in all types of conditions.

Brett's Other Interests

While kayaking is certainly a full-time job, recovery is a vital part of being an athlete. Brett's primary hobby is photography. His travels allow him to visit some of the world's most beautiful places. From Iguacu Falls to Prague he races all over the world, and the nature of training and competing give him plenty of free time to take pictures.

Being an Active Part of the Charlotte, NC Community

Athletes are known for their contributions to their local communities. Brett is no exception. While high-paid professional athletes are able to give large sums of money to support the causes that are close to their heart, Brett has to be more creative. Now that he is entering the beginning of a new Olympic cycle, he is looking for all possible ways in which he can become a more helpful member of the Charlotte community. Whether he is talking to school groups about the value of hard work and goal setting, or contributing to publications about nutrition and proper exercise, Brett embraces his position to help others live a more healthy, enjoyable and successful life through the lessons he has learned as an Elite Olympic Athlete.

  • Years of participation: K1 - Men 2002, 2003, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009; K1 Team - Men 2002, 2003, 2005, 2006, 2007, 2009

Did You Know

  • Captain of both the 2015 Pan American Cup and 2015 Pan American Games gold-medal winning teams
  • Ranked 12th in scoring and third in Best Receiver at 2015 Pan American Games
  • Ranked third in Best Spiker at 2015 Pan American Cup
  • Started 30 of 31 matches during 2013 season recording 291 points and averaging 2.94 points per set
  • Started all 14 matches of 2013 FIVB World Grand Prix averaging 3.06 points per set
  • Favorite place to play volleyball is Brazil
  • Looks up to Stacy Sykora
  • Started playing organized volleyball at age 8
  • Father, Dave, also played on the U.S. National Team from 1978 to 1980. Both Dave and her mother Lauren, who passed away in 2012, played volleyball at BYU as did her sister Lauren
  • Admires her mother most for “her strength and example she left”
  • Rehabilitated two consecutive knee surgeries in nine months
  • Married to Tyler Hildebrand, who was a setter on the U.S. Men's National Team and now spends time coaching some of USA's elite beach players
  • Favorite athlete is Karch Kiraly, her current head coach with U.S. Women's National Team
  • Two-time American Volleyball Coaches Association All-Americans at Stanford University
  • Played on U.S. Women's Junior National Team in 2001 and 2002, served as captain of 2000 U.S. Girls' Youth National Team
  • 2003 National Gatorade Player of the Year at Timpanogos High School in Orem, Utah
  • Played juniors for Players Volleyball Club, which won the 2002 Nationals

The Big Blurb
"As I got older, I understood what a special sport volleyball is because of the team aspect. The camaraderie is amazing. Throughout my career, I've been blessed to have played alongside so many great volleyball players, but even better people - many of who have become great friends." - Kristin Hildebrand tells espnW in November 2013 interview

The Quote
"It's a pretty special thing to be a part of this program. Competing alongside my inspiring teammates is something I cherish deeply. Our team has set high goals moving into the Olympic Games in Rio, and we are ready to put in the work to do everything we can to achieve them."

USA Water Polo

Quick Facts
  • Position: Push Athlete
  • Daughter of Olympic medalists, Denean Howard-Hill and Virgil Hill, Sr. 
  • Track and field athlete at Long Beach State University and University of Nevada, Las Vegas
Biographical Information

Azaria Hill grew up as the daughter of two Olympic medalists, Denean Howard-Hill in track and field, and Virgil Hill, Sr. in boxing. Hill earned a college scholarship as a sprinter at Long Beach State before finishing off her college career at UNLV, on the same team as future U.S. bobsled teammate and pilot Kaysha Love. 

Hill made her bobsled debut for Team USA in November 2023, winning her first race in two-woman bobsled as college teammate Love piloted the team to a first-place finish in the North American Cup in Lake Placid, New York. Love and Hill would finish 4th at the two-woman bobsled at the 2024 IBSF World Championships.

Hill says about her family Olympic legacy: "They never forced me or my brother and sister to do sports ... I just knew ever since I was a little girl ... I wanted to be like my mom and aunt because they were both in the Olympics.”
